title: Te Hauora o te Awa: The Health of Our River / Te Hauora o te Awa: Te Oranga o tō tātou Awa
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply rooted in the health and wellbeing of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway flowing through our rohe. Students will explore how the mauri (life force) of our awa connects directly to the hauora (health) of our Ngāti Kahungunu community, drawing on traditional knowledge of water as a taonga and its role in physical, spiritual, and environmental wellbeing.
learningObjectives: Analyze how the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku reflects the holistic hauora of our local community using the whare tapa whā model
Measure and document water quality indicators using both mātauranga Māori methods and scientific tools to assess the awa's mauri
Create and present a collaborative action plan that addresses one identified threat to the awa's health and its impact on community wellbeing
Demonstrate understanding of kaitiakitanga through practical engagement with local river restoration initiatives

activities.title: He Whakawhiti Kōrero: Stories of Our Awa
activities.description: Students will engage with local kaumātua and knowledge holders to hear pūrākau (traditional stories) and historical accounts of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They will map significant sites along the awa and identify traditional uses of the waterway for food gathering, healing, and spiritual practices, connecting these to dimensions of hauora.

activities.title: Te Tirohanga Mātauranga e Rua: Dual Lens Investigation
activities.description: Working in small groups, students will conduct field studies at designated sites along the awa. They will use both scientific water testing kits (measuring pH, clarity, nitrates) and mātauranga Māori indicators (presence of native species, water appearance and smell) to assess the current health status of the waterway.

activities.title: Te Whare Tapa Whā o te Awa: Holistic Health Analysis
activities.description: Students will create visual representations mapping their findings about the awa's health onto the four dimensions of whare tapa whā: taha tinana (physical health - water quality), taha wairua (spiritual health - cultural significance), taha hinengaro (mental health - community connection to place), and taha whānau (social health - shared responsibility).

activities.title: Mahi Kaitiaki: Practical Guardianship
activities.description: Students will participate in a hands-on restoration activity with local environmental groups, such as native planting along riverbanks, removing invasive species, or creating educational signage. They will document their contribution and reflect on how individual actions support collective wellbeing.

activities.title: He Mahere Mahi: Action Planning Hui
activities.description: Groups will develop and present actionable proposals to enhance the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Proposals must address both environmental and community health aspects, incorporating mātauranga Māori principles and modern scientific understanding.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated environmental management plans and cultural health indicators
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality monitoring resources and historical data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Local kaumātua and representatives from Manaaki Te Awa Trust for cultural guidance and traditional knowledge sharing
Water testing kits, mapping tools, and digital documentation equipment for field studies

tasks: Group presentation of water quality investigation findings using both scientific and cultural frameworks
Written reflection connecting personal experiences of kaitiakitanga to holistic hauora concepts
Action plan proposal addressing a specific challenge facing Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and its impact on community wellbeing

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A1 - Personal Health and Physical Development, A3 - Relationships with Other People
Health and Physical Education: B1 - Movement Concepts and Motor Skills, B3 - Science and Technology
Social Sciences: Understand how people manage resources and participate in communities
The Arts: Communicating and Interpreting ideas through presentation
